teaching.title: What is a Computer?
teaching.content: A computer is just dead hardware—metal, silicone, and wires. It cannot think. A program is the invisible 'ghost' inside the machine. It is a set of instructions that tells the dead hardware exactly what to do.

examples.title: Hardware vs Software
examples.explanation: If you can smash it with a hammer, it's hardware. If it's an invisible instruction in memory, it's a program.

teaching.title: The Craft
teaching.content: Programming is the act of writing that 'recipe'. But computers don't speak English. We must use a 'Programming Language'—a special bridge language that humans can read, but computers can tightly translate into electricity.

examples.title: The Bridge Language
examples.explanation: You write in C++ (readable). The compiler translates it into Machine Code (electricity).

teaching.title: Levels of Power
topics: Types of languages (low vs high level)
teaching.content: Low-level languages are like driving a manual car with raw engine wires—hard, but incredibly fast. High-level languages are like driving an automatic—easier. C++ is a unicorn: it gives you the speed of a low-level language but the organization of a high-level language.

examples.title: High vs Low
examples.explanation: High level focuses on WHAT to do. Low level perfectly manages HOW the hardware does it.
examples.code: CONCEPT: High Level (Python) vs Low Level (Assembly).

practice.explanation: Programs are immaterial instructions driving the machine.

mcq.q: Why do we need Programming Languages?
options: To bridge the gap between Human thought and Machine Code

mcq.q: What is the true act of 'Programming'?
options: Writing strict instructions for the CPU

mcq.q: Why is C++ considered so unique in terms of language levels?
options: It beautifully blends low-level memory power with high-level logic structures
It is directly understood by the CPU without a compiler

coding.title: BOSS STRIKE: Identify the Layer
coding.code:
You want to build a triple-A 3D video game engine that requires massive graphics speed.
Do you prefer a Low-level logic or High-level logic?
coding.hint: Low-Level languages talk exactly to the memory perfectly, giving you 3D speed.

teaching.title: Dumb Machines
teaching.content: Computers have ZERO common sense. If you tell a robot 'Make a sandwich,' it will crash. You must explicitly say: 1) Open fridge, 2) Grab bread, 3) Grab knife. If you miss a step, the program explodes.

examples.title: The Sandwich Crash
examples.explanation: Never assume the computer inherently knows anything.

teaching.title: The World Portals
teaching.content: How does software interact with the world? 'Input' pulls data in (Keyboard presses, mouse clicks, sensors). 'Output' blasts data out (Screens, speakers). Everything is an Input -> Output loop.

examples.title: The Infinite Loop
examples.explanation: Without input, a program is blind. Without output, it is silent.

practice.q: Pressing the 'spacebar' on a keyboard is an example of:
practice.explanation: You are sending physical data INTO the machine.

mcq.q: Why do computers crash when given vague instructions?
Hearing a beep track from a speaker is:
Swiping your finger on a mobile screen is:
A program takes a photo (___), applies a filter, and shows it on screen (___).

coding.title: BOSS STRIKE: The Broken Robot
coding.code:
'Robot, open the door!'
Why does the robot crash?
options: It doesn't have an output monitor
You didn't explicitly instruct it to walk to the door first
It is written in a High-Level language
coding.answer: You didn't explicitly instruct it to walk to the door first

teaching.title: True or False
teaching.content: At its core, computer logic is simply making a choice based on Truth. 'If it starts raining, open umbrella'. The computer checks if the statement is TRUE, then executes the action. This 1/0 branching creates all software intelligence.

examples.title: Boolean Branching
examples.explanation: All artificial intelligence boils down to massive webs of True/False (1/0) questions.
examples.code: CONCEPT: True -> Go Left. False -> Go Right.

teaching.title: The Master Plan
teaching.content: An algorithm is a finite, step-by-step master plan designed to solve a specific problem. A cooking recipe, a Rubik's cube solution, or Google's search engine are all algorithms.

examples.title: The Perfect Sequence
examples.explanation: If the steps are out of order, the algorithm fails.

teaching.title: Hunting Errors
teaching.content: You WILL make mistakes. Identifying and fixing logic mistakes is called 'Debugging'. It is 80% of a developer's job. We do it because once the code is perfect, a computer can automate thousands of tasks instantly.

examples.title: The Detective Work
examples.explanation: A bug is just a flaw in your exact logic plan. Finding it takes patience.
examples.code: CONCEPT: Fix the Logic, Fix the Bug.

practice.explanation: Algorithms are just logical recipes to achieve a goal.

mcq.q: Programs make decisions fundamentally relying on what concept?
Why is coding so incredibly useful for humanity?
options: It automates massive repetitive logic tasks instantly
It eliminates the need for math
A strictly ordered, finite set of steps

coding.title: BOSS STRIKE: The Flawed Logic
coding.code:
Step 1: Put on shoes.
Step 2: Put on socks.

What kind of error is this?
coding.hint: The steps are perfectly readable, but the order is fatally flawed!
